children.name: Locustella
children.description: Secretive streaked warblers of dense vegetation producing sustained mechanical insect-like songs. The Grasshopper Warbler can reel continuously for over two minutes. They are among the most difficult European birds to see, creeping through vegetation like small brownish mice.

children.name: Locustella naevia
children.description: A skulking, streaky warbler whose reeling, insect-like song can continue unbroken for minutes from deep within rank vegetation. It is far more often heard than seen.

children.name: Locustella fluviatilis
children.description: An eastern warbler with a rhythmic, mechanical, sewing-machine song, favouring damp scrub and waterside thickets. It reaches the western edge of its range in Sweden, where it is a scarce but regular breeder.

children.name: Locustella luscinioides
children.description: A reed-bed warbler whose buzzing, insect-like reel is lower and faster than the Grasshopper Warbler's, delivered from dense reed and sedge. A scarce breeder in southern Swedish wetlands.

children.name: Locustella lanceolata
children.description: A highly skulking warbler of dense grass and sedge, heavily streaked above and below. It breeds across the taiga zone from Scandinavia to the Russian Far East and winters in South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Locustella certhiola
children.description: A large, well-marked Locustella of marshy grasslands and reedy floodplains across Siberia, wintering in the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It has a distinctive rufous rump and well-streaked breast.

children.name: Locustella ochotensis
children.description: A large, bulky Locustella breeding in tall grasses and scrub on the coasts and islands of the Russian Far East, wintering in Southeast Asia. Named for the Sea of Okhotsk region where it is common.

children.name: Locustella pleskei
children.description: A Vulnerable species that breeds on coastal islands off Japan, Korea, and northeastern China, wintering in the Philippines and Vietnam. Population is declining due to habitat loss at breeding and wintering sites.

children.name: Locustella fasciolata
children.description: One of the largest Locustella, breeding in dense forest undergrowth and tall grass in Siberia and the Russian Far East. It winters in the Malay Archipelago and is notably secretive for its size.

children.name: Locustella davidi
children.description: A small, compact bush warbler of dense undergrowth along forest margins in central and southern Siberia, wintering in southern China and Indochina. It was named in honour of French missionary-naturalist Père Armand David.

children.name: Locustella thoracica
children.description: A montane bush warbler of the Himalayas and southwestern China, inhabiting rhododendron scrub and subalpine meadows at high elevations. Its spotted breast distinguishes it from allied species.

children.name: Locustella mandelli
children.description: A warm russet-brown bush warbler of forest undergrowth and bamboo from the Himalayas to southern China and Taiwan. It was named after Louis Mandelli, Italian tea planter and ornithologist in northeast India.

children.name: Locustella luteoventris
children.description: A plain, buff-bellied bush warbler inhabiting dense undergrowth and bracken on hillsides from the Himalayas to northern Indochina. It is resident throughout its range and rarely moves far from thick cover.

children.name: Locustella tacsanowskia
children.description: A secretive, unstreaked bush warbler of dense scrub and tall grass in central and eastern China. Named after Polish zoologist Władysław Taczanowski, it winters in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Locustella major
children.description: A large, long-billed bush warbler restricted to dense scrub along streams and in subalpine zones of the western Himalayas. It is rarely seen but detected by its loud, repeated song from deep cover.

children.name: Locustella montis
children.description: An endemic bush warbler of montane forest edges and grasslands on Java and Bali, Indonesia. It occupies dense herbage at elevations above 1,000 m and has a thin, high-pitched reeling song.

children.name: Megalurus
children.description: Large grassbirds of grasslands and reedbeds in Australasia and South and Southeast Asia. They are long-tailed, streaky brown birds that skulk through dense vegetation and are more often heard than seen.

children.name: Megalurus palustris
children.description: A large, boldly streaked grassbird of tall grasses, reedbeds, and scrub from India to the Philippines and Sulawesi. Its loud, far-carrying song is one of the most distinctive sounds of Asian wetland margins.

children.name: Megalurus punctatus
children.description: A nationally vulnerable New Zealand endemic inhabiting dense swamp vegetation and scrubland. Its distinctive filamentous tail feathers and reluctance to fly make it one of New Zealand's most secretive birds.

children.name: Megalurus gramineus
children.description: A small, streaked grassbird of freshwater and brackish swamps and reedy margins throughout mainland Australia. Its mournful, descending whistle is a characteristic sound of Australian wetlands.

children.name: Cincloramphus
children.description: Songlarks and tawny grassbirds of open grasslands and grassy woodlands in Australia and Southeast Asia. Males of the Australian songlarks perform distinctive aerial song-flights over their territories.

children.name: Cincloramphus cruralis
children.description: A large, nomadic Australian grassland bird with pronounced sexual dimorphism — the male is nearly twice the size of the female. Males perform conspicuous aerial song-flights above open grassy plains and paddocks.

children.name: Cincloramphus mathewsi
children.description: A rufous-rumped songlark of open woodlands, scrublands, and grasslands across most of mainland Australia. Males deliver a loud, rich song from exposed perches or in undulating song-flights.

children.name: Cincloramphus timoriensis
children.description: A large, tawny-streaked grassbird of tall grasslands, reedbeds, and rank herbage from the Lesser Sundas to northern and eastern Australia. It sings persistently from exposed grass stems and low shrubs.

children.name: Schoenicola
children.description: Broadtail warblers of African and Asian grasslands and marshes, characterised by their distinctively broad, graduated tails used in display. They inhabit tall grass and reed habitats.

children.name: Schoenicola brevirostris
children.description: A secretive warbler of moist grasslands and marshy areas in sub-Saharan Africa, named for its distinctively broad, fan-shaped tail spread in display. It creeps through dense grass stems and is rarely seen in the open.

children.name: Schoenicola platyurus
children.description: A Near Threatened Indian endemic restricted to tall grasslands along the Western Ghats escarpment and a few sites in the northeastern hills. Its broad tail and preference for wet grasslands make it unmistakable.

children.name: Bradypterus
children.description: Bush warblers and scrub warblers of Africa and Asia, inhabiting dense vegetation near water and in montane forests. They are difficult to observe but produce loud, distinctive songs.

children.name: Bradypterus baboecala
children.description: A common and widespread African reed warbler inhabiting dense reedbeds, bulrushes, and rank sedge along rivers, lakes, and swamps from sub-Saharan Africa to South Africa. Its loud, accelerating rattle is distinctive.

children.name: Bradypterus lopezi
children.description: A montane bush warbler of dense undergrowth and bracken-fern in the highlands of central and eastern Africa, from Cameroon to Tanzania. It is resident on isolated mountain massifs and rarely descends to lower altitudes.

children.name: Bradypterus sylvaticus
children.description: A Near Threatened South African endemic restricted to dense tangles along streams in evergreen coastal and montane forests of the southern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal, and Swaziland. It is heard more readily than seen.

children.name: Robsonius
children.description: Ground warblers endemic to the Philippines, inhabiting dense undergrowth and forest floor vegetation in montane forests of Luzon and Mindanao. They walk and hop on the ground like thrushes.

children.name: Robsonius rabori
children.description: A Vulnerable Philippine endemic restricted to montane forest floor and dense undergrowth in the Cordillera Central of Luzon. Named after Filipino ornithologist Dioscoro Rabor, it is threatened by deforestation.
